nativeRange.habitat: Deep outer reef drop-offs and reef-base hard bottoms with moderate to strong currents, typically over sand/rubble patches near shelter; adults are commonly observed in pairs hovering just above the substrate to intercept zooplankton.

diet.notes: A planktivore in the wild, feeding on zooplankton, copepods, and crustacean larvae. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and prepared frozen carnivore diets in captivity. Generally advised to offer 2–3 small feedings per day; vibrancy of the purple/violet fin coloration tends to decline on a low-quality or vitamin-poor diet.

avoidWith: other Purple Firefish (unless an established mated pair)

summary: A striking deep-water dartfish with a pale body offset by vivid purple-and-yellow fins, generally considered beginner-friendly in peaceful reef aquariums but notably more conspecific-aggressive than its cousin the common Firefish (N. magnifica).
careNotes:
The Purple Firefish (Nemateleotris decora) is actually a dartfish in family Microdesmidae (subfamily Ptereleotrinae), not a true goby — although it is closely related to gobies in order Gobiiformes, so the common name 'Purple Firefish Goby' is a misnomer. Sources generally agree it is a beginner-friendly reef fish: hardy, peaceful toward other species, and reef-safe (no documented coral or invertebrate nipping).

A tightly-sealed lid is essential. Like other dartfish, this species is a strong jumper and has been widely documented leaping from open-top tanks when startled. Most losses of this fish in the hobby are reportedly to jumps rather than to disease or aggression.

This species is more conspecific-aggressive than the related common Firefish (N. magnifica). Scott Michael notes it as the most aggressive of the Nemateleotris dartfishes — best kept singly or as a confirmed male–female mated pair. Two unrelated specimens in a single tank tend to fight, often fatally for the loser.

A minimum tank around 75 L (≈20 US gal) is commonly cited (LiveAquaria, Aquariadise); some sources allow a 10-gal nano for a single individual, but 20 gal gives more swimming room and dilutes the territorial pressure. Provide ample loose rubble and rockwork with crevices so the fish can dart into shelter — a stressed individual without a bolt-hole is more likely to jump.

In the wild this is a notably deep-water species, recorded from about 25 m down to 100 m (typically 25–70 m) on outer reef slopes from Mauritius east to Samoa, north to the Ryukyu Islands, south to New Caledonia. The collection depth is well above the strict mesophotic threshold for most specimens but does carry some decompression-stress risk for deeper-caught individuals; quarantine and slow acclimation are generally advised.

Reported lifespan is around 3 years, with 5 years achievable on a high-quality varied diet.
breedingNotes: Reported as monogamous in the wild, with bonded pairs commonly seen sharing a sand/rubble burrow. Captive breeding has not been widely documented and is considered difficult, primarily because the planktonic larvae are extremely small and require live first-feed cultures. No commonly-replicated captive-breeding protocol was found in the sources reviewed.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Confirmed 20-gal minimum, tight-fitting lid required (jumper), conspecific aggression unless mated pair, reef-safe, peaceful toward other species, diet of frozen mysis/brine shrimp + chopped seafood. Also captures the synonyms 'Decorated Firefish', 'Purple Dartfish', 'Decorated Dartfish'.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Microdesmidae, subfamily Ptereleotrinae, order Gobiiformes), max size (9.0 cm TL), native range (Mauritius to Samoa, north to Ryukyu Is., south to New Caledonia), habitat (deep outer reef drop-offs, hard bottoms with sand/rubble patches), depth range (25–100 m, usually 25–70 m), water temperature (23–27°C), and monogamous mating system. IUCN: Least Concern.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~10 cm / 4 in), care level (easy), temperament (peaceful toward other species, highly conspecific-aggressive), reef-safe (yes), diet (planktivorous carnivore — copepods/zooplankton/mysis/brine), lifespan (~3 years), water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), and jumping behavior requiring a tight lid. Source incorrectly cites family Gobiidae; FishBase's Microdesmidae was preferred.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (20 gal recommended; 10 gal floor), temperament (peaceful with other species, aggressive to own kind — house singly or as mated pair only, per Scott Michael), lifespan (3–5 years with quality diet), and jumping behavior. Aquariadise was used in lieu of Reef2Reef wiki because the wiki has no species page for N. decora.

fish.breedingNotes: Monogamous pairs; eggs are reportedly deposited in/near a sand or rubble burrow. Larvae are planktonic and very small, making first-feed rearing the main bottleneck. No widely-replicated captive-breeding protocol has been documented in the hobby sources reviewed.
